triadic_ontology.findings: Two new monographs advance the triadic-ontology program. Damiano Migliorini’s Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk (July 2025) proposes a speculative model that integrates trinitarian dogma with analytic metaphysics, arguing that divine relationality offers a path beyond substance metaphysics while preserving rational coherence. The work systematically reconstructs relation-types from Aristotle to structural realism and frames the Trinity as a “rational hypothesis” that makes relationality and multiplicity transcendental features of being. In parallel, Christopher Isabelle’s Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field (PhilArchive, April 2026) continues the Primordial Separation Ontology series by introducing the concept of the relational field as an irreducible third term required for emergence and meaning. Isabelle contends that dyadic structures alone cannot account for continuity; the relational field must  [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous research landscape is rapidly evolving with three major breakthroughs. Google's MARS framework introduces Budget-Aware Planning via cost-constrained Monte Carlo Tree Search, achieving 63% cross-branch insight transfer in AI research automation. InternAgent-1.5 demonstrates end-to-end scientific discovery across computational and empirical domains, coordinating wet lab experiments with computational modeling through unified generation-verification-evolution subsystems. Meanwhile, the Mimosa framework pioneers evolving multi-agent workflows that automatically synthesize and refine task-specific topologies, achieving 43.1% success rates on ScienceAgentBench while maintaining full auditability. On the prompt engineering front, RASPRef introduces retrieval-augmented self-supervised prompt refinement that treats prompts as optimization targets, while new guidance reveals reasoning models like GPT-5.4 Thinking and DeepSeek R1 require fundamentally different prompting strategies [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional momentum on UAP disclosure has reached fever pitch in 2026. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, chair of the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, promises a press conference to reveal materials she describes as "nonhuman origin" after viewing classified evidence in a SCIF. This follows whistleblower testimony from former intelligence officer David Grusch, who alleges a multi-decade UAP crash retrieval and reverse engineering program exists. The Pentagon's All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) faces criticism for delayed releases of UAP footage requested by Congress. President Trump has directed federal agencies to expedite UAP record releases, with the Pentagon preparing "very interesting" files for public disclosure. Meanwhile, Luis Elizondo's congressional testimony warns of a "multi-decade, secretive arms race" involving non-human technology hidden from oversight, while detailing systematic retaliation against whistleblowers who attempt disclosure.

weather: Mostly sunny today in Edmonds with highs reaching 77-80°F. Light south-southwest winds at 5-10 mph. No rain expected—just 0-10% chance. Tonight stays clear to partly cloudy, cooling to 51-53°F with light northeast winds. Perfect early May weather for outdoor activities with Piper.

local_news.findings: Construction on the 2025 sewer/storm pipe rehabilitation project begins Monday, May 4. The $934K CIPP (Cured-in-Place Pipe) program will replace aging mains on 242nd St SW, 5th Ave S, Bowdoin Way, 88th Ave W and ten other segments. Residents can expect noise, traffic control and short-term water shut-offs; work is weather-dependent and runs through mid-May.

At its April 28 meeting the council unanimously adopted the city’s first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan, a $300k roadmap that identifies 11 priority traffic-safety projects eligible for federal Safe Streets grants. Projects range from Highway 99 corridor improvements to downtown-core intersection upgrades. Adoption keeps Edmonds eligible for future grant funding; no construction dollars are yet allocated.

Police are still searching for 48-year-old Colin Boswell after a targeted, caught-on-video assault inside an Edmonds massage spa in September. Boswell allegedly used a coat rack to beat two female employees, causing broken bones, and told officers he intended to kill the victims. He fled on foot and remains at large; charges of attempted murder have been filed.

The South County Fire Board of Commissioners will hold a public hearing Monday, May 5, 7 p.m. at Fire HQ (12425 Meridian Ave S, Everett) on a proposed $420 million fire & EMS construction bond for the November ballot. If approved, funds would modernize stations serving Edmonds and southwest Snohomish County. Residents can attend in person or via Zoom (ID 876 1281 5239).

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has wrapped its 2025-26 auditions, but if Sarah wants to sing next season she should email auditions@seattlepromusica.org now to join the 2026-27 mailing list. Auditions for all voice parts are held every August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30-9:30 pm; dues are $75/yr with scholarships available. Requirements: music-reading ability, prior singing or instrumental experience, and comfort with foreign languages. Audition components: vocalizing, sight-reading two short choral excerpts in quartet, ear-repetition, and a prepared two-minute solo (art song, aria, hymn, or folk song). An accompanist is provided. The choir is ~80 voices plus three small ensembles (Vox, Chroma, Orpheon) that rehearse after the main rehearsal. All sin [TRUNCATED]
family.toddler: This week the Sno-Isle Libraries are running daily toddler storytimes. Edmonds Library (650 Main St) has Toddler Storytime with Cami on Wednesday May 7 at 10:00-10:45 am. Lynnwood Library (19200 44th Ave W) offers Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on Monday May 4 at 9:30-10:00 am and again at 10:30-11:00 am. Both programs feature stories, songs, rhymes and activities designed for ages 18 months-3 years to build early-literacy skills. No registration required—just drop in. For more options, check the full calendar at sno-isle.bibliocommons.com/events and filter for “Toddler Storytime.”

intellectual_journals.findings: Maria Popova's essay "The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You: Oliver Sacks on Perception" explores how consciousness creates reality through selective attention rather than passive reception. Drawing from Sacks' posthumous collection "The River of Consciousness," the piece examines neurological case studies where patients experience "standstills" of consciousness—moments when time appears frozen despite ongoing external events. Sacks argues that perception is not computational but creative, forming "neural coalitions" that must cross intensity thresholds to reach awareness. The [TRUNCATED]

A joint investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE reveals that over 300 protesters and bystanders arrested during Trump's immigration sweeps have seen their cases collapse under scrutiny. Federal agents routinely accused dissenters of domestic terrorism, but more than a third of cases were dismissed when video footage contradicted officer statements. The investigation documents a chilling pattern where arrests themselves serve as punishment, with prosecutors using conspiracy charges typically reserved for drug traffickers. Cases like Alejandro Orellana—a Marine veteran arrested for distributin [TRUNCATED]

Chris Maisano's "A Post-Order World" argues that American hegemony has entered terminal decline, not temporary disarray. The essay traces how US global dominance relied on "spontaneous consent" rather than mere coercion, built through post-WWII institutions that created international dependencies. This consent has now broken down as the material basis of American power—industrial supremacy and financial mechanisms—has eroded through neoliberal transformation. The piece suggests we're witnessing not an interregnum before order's restoration, but the end of the very concept of international orde [TRUNCATED]

Pankaj Mishra's "A Bitter Education" examines the historical intimacy between Iranian and Indian civilizations, using personal family history to explore how Persian culture shaped South Asian identity for nearly a millennium. The essay traces Persian's role as Asia's lingua franca, connecting political and intellectual elites across continents, while reflecting on how Western domination created shared postcolonial experiences. Mishra's pilgrimage to Hafez's tomb in Shiraz becomes a meditation on how cultural continuity persists despite political ruptures, offering a nuanced counter-narrative t [TRUNCATED]

Arang Keshavarzian's "Iran Transformed" provides crucial context for understanding Iran's Dey protests and the subsequent US-Israeli war. The essay reveals how the December 2025 uprising—sparked by bazaar merchants—represented Iran's largest internal challenge since the 1979 revolution, with protesters demanding social transformation rather than foreign intervention. Keshavarzian argues that Trump's military campaign deliberately obscured this indigenous uprising while cynically invoking it to justify regime change, pushing Iranian society toward "blood as the sole language of politics" rather [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Voice-first AI hardware has exploded into 2026 with four distinct form factors competing for necks, wrists and ears. Friend (friend.com, $7.9 M raised) is the always-listening pendant that texts you like a companion—shipping was delayed in January but units are now reaching early buyers. Omi (omi.me, $2.7 M raised) takes a productivity angle, turning every conversation into searchable "structured  [TRUNCATED]

English and History teachers now have specialized AI co-workers that do far more than generic chatbots. MagicSchool (already briefed) remains dominant, but new entrants are carving out humanities niches. Socra Education (socraeducation.com) is teacher-built for ELA and history; instead of auto-generating essays it makes student reasoning visible—teachers see a full transcript of how a learner arri [TRUNCATED]

Lifelogging wearables are splitting into two camps: camera-forward and voice-forward. The new WARE Series 100 (wareit.ai) is a stylish pendant with built-in camera and audio designed to “track what matters most.” Sandbar (sandbar.com) just unveiled Stream Ring, a conversational notepad worn as jewelry that captures thoughts in real time and passes the “bar test” for social acceptability. Nirva and [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Is the Twenty-fifth Amendment Really an Option?
newyorker.dek: After J.F.K.’s assassination, a neophyte lawyer named John Feerick was summoned to Washington to draft the provision. Now everyone wants him to weigh in on booting Trump from office. Diego Lasarte writes.
newyorker.text:
John Feerick, the eighty-nine-year-old law professor who, in 1964, wrote the bulk of the Twenty-fifth Amendment, is used to people asking him questions about the niceties of replacing a sitting President. Mostly, he dodges them. The other day, in his tidy office at Fordham Law School, he talked about the amendment’s concept of “inability,” in terms of a President’s fitness for office—but steered clear of Donald Trump. “You cannot remove powers and duties from a President without the Vice-President agreeing,” Feerick said, speaking generally. He has a full head of white hair, and wore a gray suit and orthopedic sneakers.
Feerick’s signature creation, the Twenty-fifth Amendment, has been in the news lately, most recently when President Trump was hustled out of the White House Correspondents’ dinner, after an intruder showed up with alleged plans to murder the President and top members of the Cabinet. Naturally, questions arose about the line of succession. If the gunman had been successful, Chuck Grassley, the ninety-two-year-old president pro tempore of the Senate, who skipped the dinner, would have been sworn in as President. “Half the Cabinet at least was there,” the House Speaker, Mike Johnson, who is second in line and was present at the event, said. “They’re going to have to reëvaluate this.”
Section 1 of the Twenty-fifth Amendment states that “In case of the removal of the President from office or of his death or resignation, the Vice President shall become President.” (Curiously, J. D. Vance was rushed off the stage before Trump was.)
Feerick’s involvement began in 1963, when he was a newly minted graduate of Fordham Law with a job at Skadden Arps, handling small-claims-court cases. He published an article in the Fordham Law Review about Presidential succession, an issue that had come up during the Eisenhower Administration, when the President weathered several grave illnesses. (“Eisenhower really wasn’t able to function for a good while,” Feerick said.) “I sent the article to President Kennedy, I sent it to Robert Kennedy, to Ted Kennedy,” he said. He also sent it to Arthur Krock, the venerated Times columnist, who responded with an admiring note, saying, “I wish Congress were as much interested as you and I.”
Weeks later, John F. Kennedy was assassinated, and Krock devoted his next column to Feerick’s argument. (“Clem!” Feerick called to his assistant, asking her to find the clip, amid shelves containing books he has written. “This one—‘From Failing Hands: The Story of Presidential Succession’—is out of print,” he said.)
Feerick was heartsick; he’d seen J.F.K. and his wife in a ticker-tape parade in New York. Soon, the brand-new President, Lyndon Johnson, “took an interest in the subject,” Feerick said. The article, which Feerick’s wife, Emalie, had helped with, went viral, or as viral as something could go in the sixties. Feerick was summoned to Washington to start writing what would become the Twenty-fifth. “I had to ask the law firm if they would pay me to stay overnight and take a train or plane down there,” he remembered. “I mean, I had no money. My parents were immigrants.”
Feerick spent a lot of time in D.C. One day, he said, “I got there late because the planes weren’t running. And I walked right up to the White House from the gate! I opened the door of the White House. Which I guess would be impossible today.”
Among the twelve people enlisted to work on the amendment, Feerick was the expert on Presidential vacancies. “There were eight times when Presidents died in office and Vice-Presidents succeeded,” he said. Half of these deaths were assassinations—Lincoln, Garfield, McKinley, and J.F.K. (Lincoln’s son Robert was either present at or near the first three deaths. After McKinley’s, Robert declined invitations to Presidential functions, saying that a “certain fatality” followed him.)
Feerick is also an expert in Presidential incapacity. “In conversations about drafting an amendment during Eisenhower, they talked about the ‘insanity’ of the President,” he said. Section 4 of the amendment states that the Vice-President and a majority of the Cabinet can boot a President if they determine him to be “unable to discharge the powers and duties of his office.” Before the Twenty-fifth, Presidents had informally handed over power to the Vice-President during medical issues or temporary absences, but always voluntarily. “An insane President would probably disagree with such a determination,” Feerick said.
Last month, Trump tweeted that, if Iran did not agree to his demands, “a whole civilization will die,” prompting several calls to oust him using the Twenty-fifth. Representative Jamie Raskin introduced a bill that would create a bipartisan commission of physicians, psychiatrists, and retired government officials, which could act alongside the Cabinet in the case of the amendment being invoked.
Feerick, asked to consider whether Trump’s unhinged posts, one of which included an A.I. image of himself as Jesus, might substitute for a medical diagnosis, opted to take a long view. “There are Presidents galore from the country’s history that you can find issues with their mental health,” he said. He brought up Richard Nixon. After the Watergate scandal broke, “Nixon was having a lot of difficulty, according to reports I read, and drinking and so forth.” Senior White House staff discussed invoking the Twenty-fifth as Nixon spiralled into paranoia. But Nixon resigned, sending Gerald Ford to the Oval Office.
Feerick took pains to explain that Section 4 is not designed to be a tool used by an opposition party to remove a President. The amendment, he said, was born out of logistical concerns, such as the possibility of a President becoming “unconscious for any of a number of reasons and out of reach during a crisis,” or “if his plane went down.” After Ronald Reagan was shot, Alexander Haig, the Secretary of State, ran into the press room and declared, “I am in control here in the White House!,” even though the Twenty-fifth had not been invoked, and he was actually fourth in line.
Despite the section’s wonkily procedural intentions, it is still regularly mentioned by Trump’s opponents, and Feerick is often contacted by reporters for his expertise. “I’m not getting involved in what the Vice-President should do,” he said. During Joe Biden’s Administration, when the Twenty-fifth came up in debates about whether a President can be too old, Feerick did speak up about one vital point. He remembers saying, at a panel, “Age is individual-specific.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
literary_pick.author: Junot Díaz
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2008 Pulitzer Prize and the National Book Critics Circle Award, this novel follows Oscar de León, an overweight Dominican-American 'ghetto nerd' obsessed with science fiction and fantasy, as he navigates love, family curses, and the legacy of the Trujillo dictatorship. Blending Dominican-American history with magical realism and pop culture references, the novel explores themes of identity, diaspora, and the immigrant experience in America. Critics have hailed it as a modern classic that redefined Latino literature in the 21st century.

enriched_articles.title: UFO Update: Congresswoman Promises to Show Things of 'Nonhuman Origin'
enriched_articles.text: U.S. Congresswoman Anna Paulina Luna has promised to release evidence of "things of nonhuman origin" related to UAP investigations, stating she has personally reviewed classified materials that confirm the existence of technology not made by human hands. The Florida Republican, who sits on the House Oversight Committee, said the evidence includes physical materials and craft that demonstrate capabilities beyond current human technology.

enriched_articles.title: Written Testimony of Luis Elizondo - House Oversight Committee
enriched_articles.text: Former Pentagon UAP program director Luis Elizondo testified before Congress that the U.S. government possesses "advanced technologies not made by our Government - or any other government" and that we are in the midst of a "multi-decade, secretive arms race" hidden from elected representatives. Elizondo, who ran the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program, stated that physical evidence of UAP crashes has been secretly stored by private aerospace companies for decades.

enriched_articles.title: Congressional UFO Hearing: Whistleblowers Testify About Reverse-Engineered Alien Technology and Government Retaliation
enriched_articles.text: A historic congressional hearing revealed that 95% of the government's largest UFO research program remains classified despite costing millions. Former military intelligence specialists testified about the DIA's Advanced Aerospace Weapons Systems Application Program (OSAP), describing it as "the largest acknowledged UFO program ever funded by the U.S. government" and detailing how physical evidence from UAP crashes has been secretly stored by private aerospace companies for decades.

enriched_articles.title: Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk
enriched_articles.text: Damiano Migliorini's book offers a rigorous inquiry into formulating coherent trinitarian theism through relational ontology and metaphysics grounded in the doctrine of the Trinity. The work engages with metaphysical questions surrounding the nature of being, substance, relation, and divine personhood, proposing a speculative model that integrates theological tradition with contemporary ontology while addressing the aporia between divine omniscience and human freedom.

enriched_articles.title: [PDF] The Phenomenology of Temporal Experience and the Ontology of Time
enriched_articles.text: This paper develops a phenomenological account of temporal experience that challenges traditional A-theory and B-theory approaches to time. The author argues that the subjective experience of temporal passage cannot be reduced to either tensed or tenseless facts alone, proposing instead a relational ontology of temporal experience that grounds both the phenomenology of "now" and the direction of time in fundamental physical processes.
